Part B.key_evidence: The central source is point-like in both Science and Template but appears brighter in Science, producing a compact positive residual in the Difference image with adjacent negative structure consistent with subtraction around a pre-existing source rather than a streak or edge artifact. The candidate is near a persistent cataloged source (distpsnr1 3.2 arcsec; sgscore1 0.5 is ambiguous), and ndethist=2 with very large ncovhist=844 indicates only brief activity at this fixed location.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a real astrophysical transient, specifically a supernova. The cutouts show a new/brightened point source offset from a persistent nearby source rather than clear motion between Science and Template, so the low ndethist does not strongly support an asteroid here. The event is short so far (deltajd=0), and the morphology is not that of a long-lived nucleus-centered AGN flare or an obvious stellar variable at an isolated star; instead it looks like a transient near a host/source in the reference image.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A variable star is a plausible alternative because there is a pre-existing point source in the reference and the residual is compact. However, the subtraction morphology and apparent offset from the brightest reference source make a simple stellar brightening less convincing. An asteroid is less favored because I do not see a clean Science-vs-Template positional shift of the same point source at the alert center; the signal looks tied to a fixed sky location.
